summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the founding of Oxide Computer Company with Steve Tuck and Jess Frazelle, driven by his deep conviction that hardware and software must be built together. Drawing on frustrations from running cloud infrastructure on PC-era systems and watching hyperscalers keep their superior rack-level designs to themselves, he sees an opportunity to bring integrated, hyperscaler-class infrastructure to the broader market. The company aims to serve organizations that want cloud-like experiences on their own equipment, backed by Eclipse Ventures and legendary investor Pierre Lamond.
key_points: Hardware and software should be built with each other in mind - a belief Cantrill has held since joining Sun Microsystems in the mid-1990s
Running cloud infrastructure on PC-era systems causes deep technical pain, especially from firmware operating at cross purposes with systems software
Hyperscalers have developed superior rack-level designs but keep them cloistered, while Open Compute Project hardware lacks the software to realize its potential
As SaaS companies mature from growth-focused to margin-focused, more will consider owning infrastructure rather than renting
Shared values among co-founders are paramount - more important than shared technical vision
money_quotes: Starting a computer company may sound crazy (and you would certainly be forgiven a double-take!), but it stems from a belief that I hold in my marrow: that hardware and software should each be built with the other in mind.
I was haunted by the pain and futility of trying to build a cloud with PC-era systems.
Their rack-level designs are a clear win – why are these designs cloistered among so few?
Even as the world is moving (or has moved) to elastic, API-driven computing, there remain good reasons to run on one's own equipment!

Most important of all (with the emphasis itself being a reflection of hard-won wisdom), we three share deeply-held values: we have the same principled approach, with shared aspirations for building the kind of company that customers will love to buy from – and employees will be proud to work for.
key_insight: The gap between hyperscaler-class infrastructure and what's available to everyone else represents both a technical failure and a market opportunity that can only be solved by a company willing to integrate hardware and software from the ground up.
